_meta.notes: On-prem LLM inference planning catalog. Specs current to mid-2026 where possible. Roadmap entries (e.g. Vera Rubin) carry roadmap:true and most fields null. FLOPS figures are dense (non-sparse) tensor-core throughput at the indicated precision; vendor marketing numbers frequently quote 2x sparse — those have been halved to match the dense convention. Where vendors do not publish a number at a given precision (e.g. no native FP8 on Ampere/Ada/A100), the field is set to null rather than extrapolated.

accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: 70B with 2-way TP; up to ~34B on single GPU
accelerators.notes: Production workhorse for LLM inference 2023-2025. FP8 introduced in Hopper Transformer Engine; no native FP4. Dense FP8 ~1,979 TFLOPS (sparse marketing number is 3,958).

accelerators.interconnect: NVLink bridge (600 GB/s, 2-GPU only) or PCIe Gen5
accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: ~30B on single GPU; 70B requires 2- or 4-way TP
accelerators.notes: Lower TDP and bandwidth than SXM variant; uses HBM2e instead of HBM3. Best for retrofit into standard PCIe servers without HGX baseboard.

accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: 70B on single GPU with long-context KV room; 405B with 8-way TP
accelerators.notes: Same Hopper compute envelope as H100; memory upgraded to 141 GB HBM3e at 4.8 TB/s. Sweet spot for long-context 70B serving. Per NVIDIA datasheet, dense FP8 unchanged from H100; gains are bandwidth-bound decode throughput, not compute.

accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: 70B on single GPU comfortably; 405B with 8-way TP
accelerators.typical_server: HGX B100 8-GPU (air-cooled, drop-in for HGX H100/H200)
accelerators.notes: Lower-TDP Blackwell variant intended as air-cooled drop-in for existing HGX infrastructure. Roughly 78% of B200 peak FLOPS at the same memory capacity. Some vendor figures are sparse; dense values shown here.

accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: 70B on single GPU; 405B with 8-way TP at FP8 or 4-way at FP4
accelerators.typical_server: HGX B200 / DGX B200 8-GPU (~14.3 kW, air-cooled to ~60 kW/rack; liquid above)
accelerators.notes: Flagship air-cooled Blackwell. ~2.3x FP8 throughput vs H100. Adds native FP4 (NVFP4) with 2x throughput over FP8. TDP 1000 W air / 1200 W liquid SKU. Dense FLOPS shown; NVIDIA marketing often cites 2x sparse.

accelerators.interconnect: NVLink 5.0 (1.8 TB/s per GPU); NVLink-C2C (900 GB/s GPU<->CPU)
accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: 405B comfortably on a single superchip; trillion-parameter MoE within NVL72 rack domain
accelerators.typical_server: GB200 NVL72 rack (72 B200 + 36 Grace, 120-140 kW, mandatory liquid cooling)
accelerators.notes: Per-superchip values (2x B200 + 1x Grace). Aggregated GPU memory across both Blackwell dies is 384 GB HBM3e at ~16 TB/s combined. NVL72 rack assembles 36 of these into one 72-GPU NVLink domain. Liquid cooling mandatory.

accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: 7B-13B comfortably; up to ~30B with INT4 quantization on single GPU
accelerators.notes: Workhorse for small/medium models, embeddings, retrieval, and reranking. No NVLink (PCIe-only inter-GPU). GDDR6 bandwidth ~864 GB/s is the limiter for large-model decode.

accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: Up to 7B at FP8; 13B with INT4. Best for small models, embeddings, classification
accelerators.notes: Low-power universal inference accelerator. 72 W TDP fits any rack power envelope. Bandwidth-limited for anything beyond small models.

accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: No native FP8. 13B-34B at FP16, 70B with INT4 quantization or 2-way TP at INT8
accelerators.notes: Legacy/secondary fleet. No native FP8 support (Ampere lacks Transformer Engine). Still widely deployed for batch/offline inference where SLOs are relaxed.

accelerators.interconnect: Infinity Fabric (896 GB/s per GPU, 8-GPU mesh)
accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: 70B on single GPU with KV room; 405B with 8-way TP
accelerators.typical_server: AMD Instinct MI300X 8-GPU platform / Supermicro AS-8125GS-TNMR2 (~10-11 kW)
accelerators.notes: AMD flagship for inference 2024-2025. 192 GB HBM3 is the headline advantage vs H100/H200. Software stack is ROCm; vLLM, SGLang, and TGI all support it. Dense FP8 ~2,614 TFLOPS (vendor sparse number is 5,229).

accelerators.interconnect: Infinity Fabric (896 GB/s per GPU, 8-GPU mesh)
accelerators.fits_models_at_fp8: 70B with long-context KV room on single GPU; 405B with 8-way TP at FP8 plus headroom for long context
accelerators.notes: Refresh of MI300X with HBM3e: 256 GB capacity and 6 TB/s bandwidth, same CDNA 3 compute. Largest single-GPU memory of any shipping accelerator in mid-2026.

accelerators.typical_server: Rubin NVL144 rack (announced: ~3.6 ExaFLOPS dense FP4 per rack)
accelerators.notes: FORWARD-LOOKING / UNCERTAIN. Most per-GPU specs not yet published as of mid-2026. Headline figures from NVIDIA GTC announcements only. Treat all numbers as preliminary.

networking.use_case: Intra-node tensor parallelism on Blackwell HGX/DGX baseboard
networking.notes: 1.8 TB/s per GPU aggregate, point-to-point within 8-GPU baseboard via NVSwitch. Not a network in the routed sense - confined to a single chassis (or NVL72 rack via external NVSwitch trays).

networking.use_case: Intra-node tensor parallelism on Hopper HGX/DGX baseboard
networking.notes: 900 GB/s per GPU on H100/H200 SXM. Required for sub-millisecond all-reduce in 8-GPU TP.

networking.use_case: All-to-all GPU interconnect within HGX baseboard or NVL72 rack
networking.notes: Switch silicon that enables full-bisection NVLink between all GPUs in a node or NVL72 rack. NVL72 external NVSwitch trays extend the NVLink domain across 72 Blackwell GPUs in one rack.

networking.use_case: Inter-node tensor/pipeline parallel; legacy multi-node clusters
networking.notes: 200 Gb/s per port. Common on A100-generation DGX SuperPODs. Being displaced by NDR for new builds.

networking.use_case: Inter-node tensor/pipeline parallel for Hopper- and Blackwell-class clusters
networking.notes: 400 Gb/s per port via ConnectX-7. Standard backend fabric for H100/H200 SuperPOD and equivalent OEM builds. SHARP in-network reduction supported.

networking.use_case: Inter-node fabric for Blackwell GB200 NVL72 and Rubin-class clusters
networking.notes: 800 Gb/s per port via ConnectX-8 / Quantum-X800 switches. Targeted at trillion-parameter and disaggregated prefill/decode topologies.

networking.use_case: Inter-node tensor/pipeline parallel where Ethernet is the standard fabric
networking.notes: RDMA-over-Converged-Ethernet at 400 Gb/s. Requires PFC/ECN tuning for lossless behavior. Spectrum-X / Tomahawk 5-class switches typical. Often cheaper than IB for the same raw bandwidth, at the cost of more tuning.

networking.use_case: Inter-node fabric for Blackwell/Rubin-class clusters on Ethernet
networking.notes: 800 Gb/s Ethernet with RoCE v2. Spectrum-X800 and Tomahawk 5 / 6 switches. Competes with IB XDR for new AI factory builds.

networking.use_case: Client traffic ingress, storage frontend, management, K8s pod networking
networking.notes: Standard datacenter frontend. Carries user requests, model artifacts on cold start, control plane. Distinct from the backend GPU fabric.

storage.use_case: Model weight cache, KV-cache spillover, scratch space
storage.throughput_tier: 5-14 GB/s per drive; 20-50 GB/s aggregated per node
storage.notes: PCIe Gen5 U.2/E1.S drives standard on 2026 inference servers. Critical for fast model-load on replica scale-out and for KV-cache offload tiers (Dynamo KV Block Manager, LMCACHE).

storage.use_case: Shared model repository, training/fine-tune checkpoint store, multi-node weight load
storage.notes: Examples: WEKA, VAST Data, DDN EXAScaler (Lustre), IBM Storage Scale (GPFS), Pure Storage FlashBlade. Required for fast simultaneous weight load when scaling a model across many replicas.

storage.use_case: Cold model registry, dataset archive, RAG corpus source-of-truth, KV-cache long-tail offload
storage.notes: On-prem examples: MinIO, Cloudian, Pure FlashBlade//S, Dell ECS, NetApp StorageGRID. Latency too high for hot KV cache, fine for model registry and corpus storage feeding a RAG index.

power_cooling.notes: What most legacy colocation cabinets support. Adequate for low-power inference accelerators (L4, L40S) and small PCIe nodes.

power_cooling.cooling: Containment + high-CFM air handlers, possibly in-row coolers
power_cooling.notes: Achievable in modern enterprise/colo. Above ~30 kW/rack, air alone starts to struggle on hot days.

power_cooling.cooling: Air-to-liquid rear-door coil; rack remains 'air-cooled' from the server's perspective
power_cooling.notes: Transitional tier: servers see air, the rack rejects heat to facility water via the rear door. Common path for first HGX H100/B200 deployments in existing rooms.

power_cooling.cooling: Cold plates on GPU/CPU; facility water via CDU; warm-water DLC at ~30-45 C supply common
power_cooling.notes: Required above ~80 kW/rack. Two-loop design (facility loop + technology loop via CDU) is standard. Warm-water DLC enables free cooling in many climates.

power_cooling.cooling: Direct liquid cooling, no air option; CDU per rack or per row
power_cooling.notes: Pre-integrated 72-GPU NVLink-domain rack. Cannot be air-cooled. Requires facility water delivery, structural floor loading review, and busbar power feeds rather than rack PDUs.

optimizations.name: FP16 / BF16 baseline
optimizations.memory_impact: Baseline (2 bytes per parameter for weights and KV cache)
optimizations.notes: BF16 is the de facto training baseline; FP16 still common in inference where overflow is handled. 2 bytes/param means a 70B model weights to ~140 GB before KV cache.

optimizations.name: FP8 (E4M3 / E5M2)
optimizations.quality_impact: Near-identical to BF16 for most tasks; small regressions on edge cases
optimizations.notes: Production baseline in 2026. DeepSeek-V3 trained natively in FP8; Qwen3 ships official FP8 checkpoints. Not natively supported on Ampere (A100).

optimizations.name: INT8 quantization
optimizations.quality_impact: <1% accuracy drop on standard benchmarks with calibration
optimizations.notes: Trusted workhorse: well-supported across all generations including Ampere where FP8 is absent. Frequently combined with BF16 activations.

optimizations.name: INT4 quantization (GPTQ, AWQ)
optimizations.quality_impact: Noticeable degradation on multi-step reasoning, math, code; small on chat/summarization
optimizations.notes: GPTQ and AWQ are the standard recipes. Activations typically kept at FP16/BF16 (W4A16) since activation ranges are spiky.

optimizations.name: FP4 (NVFP4)
optimizations.throughput_impact: ~2x decode throughput vs FP8 on Blackwell
optimizations.quality_impact: Close to FP8 with NVFP4 block scaling; meaningful drop with naive FP4
optimizations.notes: Native FP4 tensor-core path introduced with Blackwell. NVFP4 uses microscaled blocks (groups of 16 elements with FP8 scale) to recover quality. Not supported on Hopper or earlier.

optimizations.name: Continuous batching
optimizations.throughput_impact: Up to ~4x higher throughput vs static batching at similar latency
optimizations.notes: Dynamically admits new requests at each forward pass instead of waiting for a static batch to complete. Implemented by vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, TGI, SGLang. Hardware-agnostic - it is a serving-engine technique.

optimizations.name: PagedAttention
optimizations.memory_impact: KV-cache waste reduced from 60-80% to <4%, enabling ~4x more concurrent requests
optimizations.throughput_impact: Up to ~24x throughput vs HF Transformers naive baseline (vLLM measurement)
optimizations.notes: Treats KV cache as paged virtual memory of fixed-size blocks. Originated in vLLM, since adopted by most major serving engines.

optimizations.name: Speculative decoding
optimizations.throughput_impact: 2-3x speedup at accept rate >=0.6; up to 6-10x with distillation (DistillSpec)
optimizations.notes: Small draft model proposes N tokens, large target verifies in one pass. Works across hardware; supported in vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang. Combine with LoRA for cumulative >60% latency reduction.

optimizations.name: LoRA / multi-tenant adapters
optimizations.memory_impact: Small per adapter (MB-scale) vs full fine-tune (GB-scale); one base model serves N tenants
optimizations.throughput_impact: Near-baseline with per-request adapter swap; slight overhead for adapter merge
optimizations.quality_impact: Equal to fully fine-tuned model for the adapter's task domain
optimizations.notes: vLLM Multi-LoRA, S-LoRA, Punica patterns. Lets one cluster host many fine-tunes instead of N clusters.

optimizations.name: Prefix caching
optimizations.memory_impact: Neutral to small additional KV resident; reuse across requests
optimizations.throughput_impact: Prefill cost reduced 40-70% on workloads with shared system prompts / RAG retrieval patterns
optimizations.notes: Reuses KV cache for shared prompt prefixes (e.g. system prompt, RAG document chunks). Critical for chatbots and agent workflows. Implemented in vLLM (APC), SGLang (RadixAttention), TensorRT-LLM.

serving_engines.name: vLLM
serving_engines.primary_use: General-purpose open-source LLM serving on NVIDIA and AMD GPUs
serving_engines.notes: De facto open-source default. Originated PagedAttention. ROCm support is first-class for MI300X/MI325X.

serving_engines.name: NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM
capabilities: Compiled engine specialization per model + GPU
serving_engines.primary_use: Highest-throughput NVIDIA-only serving when compile-time specialization is acceptable
serving_engines.notes: NVIDIA-only. Generally edges vLLM on raw throughput but requires per-model engine builds. Pairs with Triton for production serving.

serving_engines.name: NVIDIA Triton Inference Server
capabilities: Multi-framework backend (TensorRT-LLM, vLLM, PyTorch, ONNX, custom)
serving_engines.primary_use: Production serving frontend that hosts model backends (often TensorRT-LLM or vLLM)
serving_engines.notes: Not an LLM runtime itself - hosts a runtime backend. Standard way to deploy TensorRT-LLM in production. CPU and AMD backends exist but NVIDIA is the primary target.

serving_engines.name: Hugging Face Text Generation Inference (TGI)
serving_engines.license: Apache-2.0 (HFOIL terms apply to some commercial uses)
serving_engines.primary_use: Hugging Face ecosystem serving; easy on-ramp for HF Hub models
serving_engines.notes: Tight integration with HF Hub and tokenizers. Supports NVIDIA, AMD ROCm, and (more recently) Intel Gaudi.

serving_engines.name: SGLang
serving_engines.primary_use: High-throughput serving with strong prefix-caching and structured-output workloads (agents, tool-use, JSON)
serving_engines.notes: Strong choice for agent and structured-output workloads where prefix reuse dominates. RadixAttention is its differentiator.

serving_engines.name: NVIDIA Dynamo
capabilities: KV Block Manager (multi-tier KV offload: GPU / CPU / NVMe / object)
serving_engines.primary_use: Datacenter-scale disaggregated inference orchestration across many nodes
serving_engines.notes: Successor framing to Triton for the AI-factory / NVL72 era. Manages a fleet of prefill workers, decode workers, and KV tiers - not a single-node runtime.
